title: Short
article:
Short
2020-12-28/28th December 2020

This article has been left deliberately short in order to test how shorter articles are handled by the software that builds this site.

title: A differently specified subset of Markdown
article:
A differently specified subset of Markdown
2020-12-26/26th December 2020

Yes, I've written my own Markdown parser. This is mostly because ...

# I want to write it myself (I can still do code!)
# I don't want to have to depend on any third-party library, no matter how small and efficient it is.
# I only intend to use a handful of HTML tags (<a>, <strong>, <ul>, etc.) so I don't need a complete parser. I will unapologetically use a differently specified subset of Markdown.

Tag support currently runs to ...

<em> tags with _
<strong> tags with *
<ol> done with # as the first char on each line
<ul> done with * as the first char on each line
<a> done with ^ where the link text and the href are separated by a |

title: First
article:
First
2020-12-16/16th December 2020

Go back far enough on any blog and you'll eventually get to the first post. This is mine. It doubles as a rudimentary test of the "markdown" parser, so we can test that it will do things like _emphasis_ and *strong text passages*.

This line has a _ inserted for fun. Hopefully the parser will leave it alone and not render the rest of the article in italics. We should be able to leave stray * in there too ...

_This one_ has *a little* bit of _ everything

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur. Excepteur sint occaecat cupidatat non proident, sunt in culpa qui officia deserunt mollit anim id est laborum.

Ordered lists are also desirtable.

# This will be a list wrapped in <ol> tags.
# And so will this be
# and this one.

And the list is done.
# but here's a new one!

# Can we start a new list after a blank line?
* Can we start an _unordered_ list on the next line? Let's hope so!

Now it's' time to think about how ^hyperlinks|http://www.google.com/^ might work. Or ^hyperlinks with *styled _text_* in them|http://www.cnn.com/^
